reasoning: FALSE POSITIVE. The finding contains two material errors that together falsify it. (1) Hook attribution: DGWT_WCAS_WC_AJAX_ENDPOINT is defined as true in ajax-search-for-woocommerce.php:247, so the if-branch at Search.php:112 is taken (wc_ajax_dgwt_wcas_ajax_search), and the else-branch at lines 115-116 (wp_ajax_nopriv_) is dead code in all normal deployments. The reachability claim is still correct in substance — wc_ajax_ is equally unauthenticated — but the cited code path is wrong. (2) product-ids context claim is false: $context is a PHP function parameter with default 'autocomplete'; it is never read from $_REQUEST. When WordPress invokes the AJAX hook it calls getSearchResults() with no arguments, so $context='autocomplete' always. An attacker cannot supply context=product-ids via HTTP to reach that branch — it is only exercised by an internal programmatic call at Search.php:770 with $return=true. The autocomplete path (the only one reachable externally) queries only post_status=publish products and applies the getTaxQuery() which excludes exclude-from-search products — returning identical data to what any visitor sees by browsing the shop. SKU, price, and description are gated behind per-setting checks (show_product_sku, show_product_price, show_product_desc). There is no nonce check (confirmed: //phpcs:disable WordPress.Security.NonceVerification.Recommended at line 178), but absence of a nonce on a read-only public search widget is by design, not a vulnerability. No unpublished products, no draft content, and no admin-only fields are exposed. The missing_authentication label is a category mismatch: this is an intentionally public autocomplete endpoint, not a guarded action.
